steps.title: Focus on valuation prompts
steps.content: The passage says sellers can benefit by prompting consumers to consider their own valuations. In the graph, the message "pay what you think it's worth" is the condition that directly asks participants to consider ticket value.

steps.title: Compare the graph results
steps.content: For both groups, the valuation prompt produces the highest mean ticket price: about 55 dollars for the online group and about 74 dollars for the students. The student group also assigns a higher value than the online group, showing heterogeneous valuations.

steps.title: Connect data to the claim
steps.content: Those two observations match the passage's wording. Different mean prices between the two groups illustrate heterogeneous consumer valuations. Higher prices under the valuation prompt show why sellers can benefit when purchasers are asked to think about what the tickets are worth.

steps.title: Reject unsupported details
steps.content: Choice A reverses the "pay what you can" comparison because students chose about 40 dollars, higher than the online group's about 30 dollars. Choice B mentions actual ticket cost, which the graph does not provide. Choice D claims wide individual variation within each group, but the graph shows mean prices, not individual spread. Choice C captures both patterns: students valued the tickets more highly, and both groups paid more under the valuation prompt. The answer is C.
